title: Why 43% of Physiotherapy Care Is Non-Recommended — and What Clinics Can Do About It
meta_description: Research shows nearly half of physiotherapy care doesn't follow evidence-based guidelines. Learn why this happens and practical steps to improve clinical consistency.
html_content:
The figure is stark: up to 43% of physiotherapy care delivered in clinical practice doesn't align with evidence-based recommendations. This isn't about incompetent clinicians or deliberate disregard for research. It's about the structural challenges that make consistent, evidence-based practice difficult to implement and maintain in real-world clinical settings.

Understanding why this gap exists — and more importantly, what clinics can do to close it — requires examining the specific barriers that prevent physiotherapists from delivering care that matches research evidence. The solutions aren't about working harder; they're about working more systematically.

The Evidence-Practice Gap in MSK Physiotherapy

The problem isn't that physiotherapists don't know the research. Most clinicians are well-versed in current evidence and guidelines. The issue is translating that knowledge into consistent practice patterns when faced with heavy caseloads, time pressures, and the inherent variability of clinical presentation.

Take ACL rehabilitation, where evidence strongly supports structured, progressive loading protocols. Recent systematic reviews demonstrate that specific exercise progressions and objective milestones significantly improve outcomes (Fontanier et al., 2025). Yet many clinics still rely on time-based progressions rather than criterion-based advancement, leading to premature return-to-sport clearances or unnecessarily prolonged treatment.

The gap becomes more pronounced in common conditions where treatment protocols appear straightforward but require precise execution. Shoulder impingement provides a clear example: research shows that combining corticosteroid injection with structured physiotherapy produces superior outcomes compared to either intervention alone (Hsieh et al., 2023). However, without standardised assessment protocols, clinicians often can't reliably identify which patients will benefit from combined treatment versus physiotherapy alone.

Three primary factors drive non-recommended care:

  • Assessment inconsistency: Over 90% of physiotherapists rely on subjective strength testing, making it difficult to apply research-based treatment thresholds
  • Progression uncertainty: Without objective markers, clinicians often default to time-based rather than criterion-based treatment advancement
  • Outcome variability: Inconsistent measurement makes it difficult to identify when treatments aren't working as expected

Why Standardised Protocols Fail Without Measurement Infrastructure

Most attempts to improve evidence-based practice focus on developing better protocols or providing more education. These approaches miss a fundamental point: protocols are only as good as the measurement systems that support them. Without reliable, objective assessment tools, even the best protocols become subject to interpretation and inconsistent application.

Consider rotator cuff repair rehabilitation, where systematic reviews clearly outline evidence-based progression criteria (Bandara et al., 2021). The research specifies when to initiate passive range of motion, progress to active movement, and advance to resistance training. But these recommendations depend on objective assessment of tissue healing, range of motion, and strength capacity. Without standardised measurement, clinicians often rely on subjective assessment, leading to either overly cautious or inappropriately aggressive progression.

The same pattern emerges across conditions. Blood flow restriction training shows significant promise for accelerating rehabilitation outcomes (Hughes et al., 2017), but its application requires precise pressure monitoring and objective strength assessment to determine appropriate resistance levels. Digital physiotherapy platforms demonstrate comparable outcomes to conventional treatment for chronic conditions (Pak et al., 2023), but only when they incorporate objective measurement and standardised progression criteria.

The key insight is that evidence-based protocols require measurement-based implementation. Clinical guidelines provide the framework, but objective assessment tools provide the precision necessary for consistent application.

The Clinical Decision-Making Problem

Non-recommended care often results from decision-making under uncertainty. When clinicians can't reliably measure key variables — strength, function, pain levels, movement quality — they're forced to make treatment decisions based on incomplete information. This leads to predictable patterns of over-treatment, under-treatment, and inappropriate treatment selection.

The ACL reconstruction literature illustrates this clearly. Comparative studies show that non-surgical management can produce outcomes comparable to surgery when applied to appropriate patients (Beard et al., 2024). However, identifying these appropriate patients requires objective assessment of knee function, quadriceps strength, and activity demands. Without standardised measurement protocols, clinicians often default to surgical referral rather than attempting conservative management.

Similarly, post-surgical rehabilitation suffers from the same measurement gaps. Research demonstrates that early physiotherapy significantly improves outcomes after total knee arthroplasty (Aftab et al., 2025), but optimal treatment intensity and progression depend on objective assessment of range of motion, strength, and functional capacity. Without these measurements, clinicians often apply one-size-fits-all protocols that may be too aggressive for some patients and insufficient for others.

The solution isn't better clinical intuition — it's better measurement infrastructure. When clinicians have access to reliable, objective data, their clinical decision-making improves dramatically. The combination of clinical experience and objective measurement produces more consistent, evidence-based treatment decisions.

Building Measurement-Based Practice Systems

Creating more consistent, evidence-based practice requires systematic changes to how clinics approach assessment and treatment progression. This isn't about replacing clinical judgment — it's about providing the data infrastructure that makes clinical judgment more reliable and defendable.

Start with standardising key assessments across your clinic. Rather than each clinician using their preferred strength testing methods, implement consistent protocols for the most common conditions you treat. For shoulder conditions, this might include standardised external rotation testing at 0° and 90° abduction. For knee conditions, implement consistent quadriceps strength testing using manual resistance techniques that can be quantified.

Next, establish objective progression criteria rather than relying solely on time-based protocols. Research consistently shows that criterion-based progression produces better outcomes than time-based advancement. For meniscus repair rehabilitation, systematic reviews demonstrate that objective functional milestones predict better long-term outcomes than adherence to time-based restrictions (Harput et al., 2020).

Key implementation steps:

  1. Identify your clinic's most common conditions and select 2-3 objective tests for each that align with research evidence
  2. Train all clinicians in standardised administration of these tests to ensure consistent results across your team
  3. Establish progression thresholds based on research evidence rather than clinical tradition
  4. Track outcomes systematically to identify when treatments aren't producing expected results
  5. Review cases regularly where outcomes don't match expectations to refine your protocols

Making Evidence-Based Care Sustainable

The goal isn't perfect adherence to every research recommendation — it's creating systems that make evidence-based care the path of least resistance. This requires addressing the practical barriers that push clinicians toward non-recommended care patterns.

Time pressure is often cited as the primary barrier, but the real issue is inefficient assessment and decision-making processes. When clinicians spend 10 minutes per session on subjective strength testing that provides unreliable data, they're using time inefficiently. Implementing standardised, objective tests that take the same amount of time but provide reliable data improves both efficiency and clinical accuracy.

Documentation and communication also improve with standardised measurement. Rather than writing subjective descriptions of patient progress, clinicians can report objective changes in strength, range of motion, or functional capacity. This makes treatment justification more straightforward and patient communication more precise.

Most importantly, measurement-based systems provide the feedback necessary for continuous improvement. When outcomes don't match expectations, objective data helps identify whether the issue is assessment accuracy, treatment selection, or progression timing. This creates a learning system that improves over time rather than perpetuating inconsistent practices.

Start Measuring What Matters

Closing the evidence-practice gap in physiotherapy requires more than good intentions or additional training. It requires systematic changes to how clinics approach assessment, treatment selection, and outcome measurement. The infrastructure needs to support evidence-based decision-making, not work against it.

Benchmark PS addresses this challenge by providing standardised, objective assessment tools that integrate seamlessly into existing practice patterns. Rather than expensive equipment or time-consuming protocols, the platform uses simple, research-validated tests that provide the objective data necessary for consistent, evidence-based treatment decisions.

The result is physiotherapy that aligns with research evidence not through additional effort, but through better measurement infrastructure. When clinicians have access to reliable, objective data, delivering evidence-based care becomes the natural choice rather than an additional burden.

faq.q: Why is so much physiotherapy care non-recommended despite good clinical training?
faq.a: The problem isn't knowledge but implementation. Without objective measurement tools, clinicians can't reliably apply research-based treatment thresholds and progression criteria, leading to inconsistent care patterns.

faq.q: How can small clinics implement evidence-based protocols without expensive equipment?
faq.a: Focus on standardising simple, manual assessment techniques across your team. Consistent application of basic strength and functional tests provides more reliable data than expensive equipment used inconsistently.

faq.q: What's the most important change clinics can make to improve evidence-based practice?
faq.a: Replace time-based treatment progressions with criterion-based advancement using objective measurements. Research consistently shows this approach produces better outcomes across most MSK conditions.

original_topic: Why 43% of Physiotherapy Care Is Non-Recommended — and What Clinics Can Do About It
